title: Worldliness, Humility, and Dependence on God
subtitle: The source of quarrels, friendship with the world, and submitting to God

sections.header: Verses 1-10 - "Submit to God, Resist the Devil"

heb.paragraph: Your passions are at war within you. James traces conflict to internal desires.
You adulteresses! Friendship with the world is enmity with God.
Humble yourselves before the Lord, and he will exalt you.

panels.ctx: James diagnoses the source of community conflict: unchecked desires. The solution is radical: submit to God, resist the devil, draw near to God.

cross.note: Do not love the world or the things in the world.
God opposes the proud but gives grace to the humble.
The LORD mocks the proud but gives favor to the humble.

notes.note: Quarrels come from passions at war within. You do not have because you do not ask.
Friendship with the world is adultery against God. But He gives more grace.
Ten imperatives: submit, resist, draw near, cleanse, purify, mourn, humble.
Calvin sees James exposing the root of social discord: disordered desire.
God is jealous for His people. The solution to worldliness is repentance.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: You murder is startling. James may use murder hyperbolically.
The quotation is difficult. James may paraphrase the general OT teaching.

moo.source: Douglas Moo, Pillar James — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Moo sees this as the letter climax. The problem is worldliness.

mccartney.source: Dan McCartney, BECNT James — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: McCartney connects this to the double-minded person of 1:8.

panels.hist: James writes to 'the twelve tribes in the Dispersion' — Jewish Christians scattered throughout the Roman Empire after persecution in Jerusalem (Acts 8:1; 11:19). The letter reflects the earliest stage of Christianity when believers still gathered in synagogues (2:2, Greek synagōgē). James, the brother of Jesus, led the Jerusalem church (Acts 15; Gal 2:9) until his martyrdom in AD 62. His letter addresses practical concerns: economic disparity between rich and poor, the relationship between faith and works, and the power of the tongue. The wisdom tradition of Judaism pervades the letter, echoing Proverbs and Jesus' Sermon on the Mount. The agricultural imagery (1:11; 3:12; 5:7) and references to early and late rains reflect the Palestinian context.

sections.header: Verses 11-17 - "Do Not Judge; Do Not Boast"

heb.paragraph: Do not speak evil against one another. Slandering a brother is setting oneself above the law.
What is your life? You are a mist that appears and then vanishes.
You boast in your arrogance. All such boasting is evil.

panels.ctx: James addresses two sins: judging brothers and presumptuous planning. Life is a vapor. Right planning acknowledges if the Lord wills.

cross.note: Judge not, that you be not judged.
The parable of the rich fool.

notes.note: Speaking evil against a brother is judging the law. There is one Lawgiver and Judge.
Come now, you who say: Today or tomorrow we will go. Life is a mist.
Whoever knows the right thing to do and fails to do it, for him it is sin.
Calvin notes that judging others usurps God prerogative.
Presumptuous planning ignores human frailty and divine sovereignty.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: Speaking against the law means treating the law as inadequate.
If the Lord wills became a common Christian expression.

moo.source: Douglas Moo, Pillar James — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Moo sees both sections addressing arrogance. Humility is the antidote.

mccartney.source: Dan McCartney, BECNT James — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: McCartney connects this to the wisdom theme. Planning without God is earthly wisdom.

themes.note: Sovereignty dominates: God opposes the proud, gives grace to the humble.

rows.label: vv. 1-6
rows.text: Source of quarrels; friendship with the world

lit.note: Chapter 4 exposes worldliness and arrogance. Both require submission to God.
